title: CLAUDE_MEM_TIER_SUMMARY_MODEL ignored when summarize is queued to running generator
author: Azhi-ss
body:
## Summary

`CLAUDE_MEM_TIER_SUMMARY_MODEL` is configured but summary generation still uses the default model (`deepseek-v4-flash`) instead of the configured tier model (`deepseek-v4-pro-260425`).

## Environment

- claude-mem version: 13.11.0
- Provider: openrouter
- OS: Linux (WSL2)

## Configuration

```json
{
  "CLAUDE_MEM_TIER_ROUTING_ENABLED": "true",
  "CLAUDE_MEM_TIER_SIMPLE_MODEL": "deepseek-v4-flash-260425",
  "CLAUDE_MEM_TIER_SUMMARY_MODEL": "deepseek-v4-pro-260425",
  "CLAUDE_MEM_MODEL": "sensenova_deepseek-v4-flash",
  "CLAUDE_MEM_OPENROUTER_MODEL": "sensenova_deepseek-v4-flash"
}
```

## Expected behavior

When a session ends and the worker processes a `summarize` message, `applyTierRouting()` should set `modelOverride` to `CLAUDE_MEM_TIER_SUMMARY_MODEL` and the SDK call should use `deepseek-v4-pro-260425`.

## Actual behavior

Every summary request still logs:

```
[SDK   ] OpenRouter API usage {model=deepseek-v4-flash, ...}
```

The configured `CLAUDE_MEM_TIER_SUMMARY_MODEL` is never used.

## Evidence

With `CLAUDE_MEM_LOG_LEVEL=DEBUG`, the summarize request is logged as:

```
[2026-07-14 12:41:10.419] [DEBUG] [HTTP  ] → POST /api/sessions/summarize requesting summary
[2026-07-14 12:41:10.421] [INFO ] [QUEUE ] [session-532] ENQUEUED | sessionDbId=532 | messageId=5 | type=summarize | depth=2
```

The queue depth is `2`, meaning the generator is already running when the summarize message arrives. The summary is eventually stored:

```
[2026-07-14 12:41:33.208] [INFO ] [DB    ] [session-532] STORED | ... | summaryId=354
```

But the SDK call immediately before it is:

```
[2026-07-14 12:41:33.610] [INFO ] [SDK   ] OpenRouter API usage {model=deepseek-v4-flash, inputTokens=41414, outputTokens=1128, totalTokens=42542, messagesInContext=65}
```

No `Tier routing: summary model` debug message appears in the logs.

## Root cause analysis

Looking at `scripts/worker-service.cjs`, `applyTierRouting()` checks the session message buffer for a `summarize` message:

```js
let i=s.some(a => a.message_type === "summarize")
if (i) {
  let a = n.CLAUDE_MEM_TIER_SUMMARY_MODEL;
  a && (r.modelOverride = a, h.debug("SESSION", "Tier routing: summary model", {sessionId: r.sessionDbId, model: a}))
}
```

However, when the summarize request is enqueued while the generator is already processing earlier messages, the `summarize` message appears to be consumed from the buffer before `applyTierRouting()` is evaluated, so the condition is never true and `modelOverride` is never set.

## Reproduction steps

1. Configure `CLAUDE_MEM_TIER_ROUTING_ENABLED=true` and `CLAUDE_MEM_TIER_SUMMARY_MODEL=deepseek-v4-pro-260425`.
2. Restart the worker.
3. Start a session, perform a few tool calls so the generator is running.
4. End the session to trigger `/api/sessions/summarize`.
5. Observe that the SDK API usage still shows the default flash model, not the configured summary model.

## Suggested fix

Ensure `applyTierRouting()` is evaluated with the current message's type visible, or route summarize messages at dequeue time rather than only when the generator starts.

title: Antigravity GUI IDE: no automatic transcript capture (only agy CLI hooks are wired)
author: honbou
body:
### Summary

The `--ide antigravity` integration only wires the **Antigravity CLI (`agy`)** via Gemini-shared hooks. Users of the **Antigravity GUI IDE** (the VS Code–based app, `~/Library/Application Support/Antigravity`) get **no automatic transcript capture**, because the GUI does not execute the `~/.gemini/settings.json` hooks that the installer writes.

### What the installer does today

`npx claude-mem install --ide antigravity` (v13.11.0) writes:
- Hooks into `~/.gemini/settings.json` (`SessionStart`/`BeforeAgent`/`AfterAgent`/`BeforeTool`/`AfterTool`/`Notification`/`PreCompress` → `bun worker-service.cjs hook antigravity-cli …`)
- Context file `~/.gemini/GEMINI.md`
- MCP config in `~/.gemini/antigravity/mcp_config.json` and `~/.gemini/config/mcp_config.json`

`npx claude-mem antigravity-cli status` reports everything as installed (7/7 events mapped).

### The gap

- Those hooks fire only for the **Gemini/Antigravity CLI (`agy`)** runtime. The `agy` binary is not necessarily installed, and the GUI IDE never runs these hooks.
- Result: for GUI users, `sdk_sessions.platform_source` never records an `antigravity` origin — zero observations are captured. The MCP tools are reachable for manual use, but there is no passive transcript capture (effectively MCP-only, like copilot-cli).

### Expected

Either:
1. A native GUI integration for the Antigravity IDE (equivalent to the opencode plugin — hook the IDE's agent/tool lifecycle and forward to the worker), or
2. Documentation making explicit that `--ide antigravity` covers only the `agy` CLI and that the GUI IDE has no automatic capture (so users don't assume it works after `status` shows green).

### Environment

- claude-mem 13.11.0, macOS (Darwin arm64)
- Antigravity GUI IDE (VS Code fork)
- Worker runtime, provider=claude

title: Worker frequently "did not become healthy before timeout" on any respawn (cold start / crash / concurrent launch), causing 60s UserPromptSubmit hook timeouts (not just upgrades)
author: LeBwa1221
body:
## Before submitting

- [x] I searched [existing issues](https://github.com/thedotmack/claude-mem/issues) and confirmed this is not a duplicate

## 📝 Manual Bug Report

### Bug Description
The claude-mem background worker frequently fails to become healthy when it (re)spawns, and callers wait out their timeout. This shows up two ways:

- **`UserPromptSubmit` `session-init` hook** — killed at Claude Code's 60s hook ceiling, adding a ~60s stall at session start (and skipping memory injection that time).
- **MCP-client worker auto-start** — not bounded by the 60s hook limit, so it can hang far longer (observed up to ~35 minutes), during which worker-backed MCP tools fail.

The common log signature is:
```
Live PID detected but worker did not become healthy before timeout
```
A `worker.pid` exists, but the worker isn't actually answering on its port, so launchers block instead of quickly detecting a dead/slow worker.

Over ~3 months of logs (69 daily files), 18 discrete timeout incidents occurred. **Only 5 were near a plugin version change; 13 (72%) had no version change at all** — they were cold starts, worker crashes, or concurrent spawn races. So this is not upgrade-specific.

A contributing factor on Windows: the worker port is derived from `process.getuid()`, which is **undefined on Windows** (POSIX-only API — Windows uses SIDs, not numeric UIDs), so it falls back to a constant and **every profile computes the same port, 37777**, sharing one worker and one data dir (`~/.claude-mem`). This maximizes contention — multiple profiles' hooks and MCP servers all race to auto-start/reach the single shared worker.

### Steps to Reproduce
Any of these reliably produce a timeout (none require an upgrade):
1. **Concurrent spawn race** — open multiple Claude Code profiles/windows (all share port 37777 on Windows); when the worker isn't already up, their hooks and MCP servers race to auto-start it and lose the handoff. (Seen repeatedly, e.g. an MCP client reconnect `{reason=stdio-closed}` → auto-start → `did not become healthy before timeout`.)
2. **Cold start** — first session of the day / after reboot, worker spawns but doesn't open its port within the ~15s cold-boot budget.
3. **Crash / stale worker** — a `worker.pid` points at a dead or unresponsive worker; auto-start returns false and the condition persists for many minutes.
4. **Version upgrade** — plugin updates while an older worker is running; all profiles detect the mismatch and race to recycle/respawn.

### Expected Behavior
Worker (re)spawn should be robust: a stale/unresponsive PID is detected quickly, a single launcher wins and the others poll its actual health endpoint (not a fixed timer), and cold starts complete without multi-minute or 60s stalls.

### Environment
- **Claude-mem version**: 13.11.0 (issue observed across multiple versions back to 13.4.x)
- **Claude Code version**: 2.1.207
- **OS**: Windows 11 (10.0.26200)
- **Platform**: win32

### Logs
From `~/.claude-mem/logs/`. Representative incidents across categories:

Concurrent spawn race, no upgrade (2026-06-22, one of six that morning):
```
05:43:58  Claude-mem search server started
05:43:59  Worker not available, attempting auto-start for MCP client
05:44:05  Live PID detected but worker did not become healthy before timeout
(no version-mismatch line anywhere in that day's log)
```

Crash / stale worker, no upgrade (2026-05-18, persisted ~35 min):
```
04:44:14  Live PID detected but worker did not become healthy before timeout
          ERROR Worker auto-start returned false — MCP tools that require the worker will fail until the worker is running
```

Version-upgrade case (2026-07-13):
```
06:05:04  Worker version mismatch — recycling stale worker {pluginVersion=13.11.0, workerVersion=13.10.2}
06:15:11  ERROR ✗ Worker failed to start — Failed to start server. Is port 37777 in use?
```

### Additional Context
**Proof `process.getuid` is undefined on Windows** (Node v22.16.0):
```
> node -e "console.log('getuid type:', typeof process.getuid)"
getuid type: undefined
> node -e "console.log(37700 + ((process.getuid?.() ?? 77) % 100))"
37777
```
`~/.claude-mem/worker.pid` reports `"port": 37777`, and `127.0.0.1:37777` is the only listening worker socket, shared across all profiles.

**Suggested fixes**
1. Make the spawn-lock handoff poll the winning worker's actual health endpoint instead of a fixed cold-boot timer, and detect a stale/unresponsive PID quickly so callers fail fast instead of waiting out 60s (or minutes).
2. Isolate the worker per profile — derive the port and lock from a hash of `CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR` rather than the `getuid`-based constant (which collapses to one port on Windows, and isn't per-profile even on POSIX). This removes the cross-profile contention that drives the concurrent-spawn-race case.
3. Recover cleanly from a crashed/stale worker rather than leaving the "auto-start returned false" condition persisting for tens of minutes.

title: chroma-mcp subprocesses leak and are never reaped — 1000+ orphaned procs / ~48 GB RAM after a day of Claude Code sessions
author: joebitcoinorg
body:
### Summary
claude-mem spawns a **new persistent `chroma-mcp` subprocess per memory operation** (via `uvx`) and never terminates it. When the spawning process exits, each `chroma-mcp` **reparents to PID 1 and lingers forever**. Over a day of normal Claude Code usage these accumulate without bound until the machine runs out of memory.

### Impact / Severity — **High**
After ~11 hours across ~9 Claude Code sessions on one workstation:
- **1,057 leaked `chroma-mcp` processes**
- **~48 GB resident RAM** consumed by them
- The box (62 GB, **no swap**) saturated to <1 GB free → **GNOME/KDE desktop sessions hard-crashed** repeatedly
- Steady-state leak rate **~2–6 new orphans/minute even with a single active session**

Killing them all reclaimed ~45 GB instantly (55 GB → 10 GB used) with no loss of function (a fresh `chroma-mcp` spawns on the next op).

### Environment
- claude-mem **v13.11.0** (also reproduced on 13.10.3 / 13.10.4)
- Claude Code CLI (plugin runtime, bun-spawned)
- Ubuntu 24.04.4 LTS, kernel 6.11.0
- `chroma-mcp==0.2.6`, launched via `uv` / `uvx`

### The leaking process
Each spawn is:
```
uv tool uvx --python 3.13 --with onnxruntime>=1.20 --with protobuf<7 \
  --from chroma-mcp==0.2.6 chroma-mcp --client-type persistent \
  --data-dir ~/.claude-mem/chroma
```
After the spawner exits these show `PPID = 1` (orphaned) and never terminate.

### Steps to reproduce
1. Install claude-mem (server runtime + mcp-search plugin) and use Claude Code normally for an extended period (many tool calls / several sessions).
2. Watch the count grow:
   ```
   pgrep -fc "chroma-mcp --client-type persistent"     # climbs continuously
   ps -eo rss,args | grep chroma-mcp | awk '{s+=$1} END{print s/1024 " MB"}'
   ```
3. Most are orphaned to PID 1 and never reaped; total RSS grows unbounded.

### Root cause (hypothesis)
The mcp-search path spawns a **fresh** `chroma-mcp` per operation instead of reusing a single long-lived client, and there is no teardown when the parent (bun/session) exits — so they orphan and accumulate.

### Expected behavior
Either (a) **reuse a single, long-lived `chroma-mcp` process** (singleton / connection pool) across operations, or (b) **terminate the `chroma-mcp` subprocess when its operation/session completes** (and on plugin shutdown). Orphaned helpers should never be left running.

### Workaround (for others hitting this)
A cron reaper that kills only **orphaned** (`PPID==1`) `chroma-mcp` every minute — active ones are spared:
```bash
for pid in $(pgrep -f "chroma-mcp --client-type persistent"); do
  [ "$(ps -o ppid= -p "$pid" | tr -d ' ')" = "1" ] && kill -9 "$pid"
done
```

Happy to provide more diagnostics (full `ps` dumps, timing) if useful. Thanks for the tool!

title: Windows/Git Bash: hooks exit 2 with misleading 'printf: write error: Permission denied'; worker can't self-heal (stale PID file + Bun not on hook PATH)
author: syamilsahimi99-cell
body:
## Environment

- claude-mem **13.2.0** (plugin install via marketplace cache)
- Windows 10 Home 10.0.19045, Git Bash (MSYS bash 5.3.9 — `/usr/bin/bash` invoked by the hooks)
- Node v24.15.0 on PATH; **Bun installed at `~/.bun/bin/bun.exe` but not on PATH**
- Claude Code with the standard plugin hooks from `hooks/hooks.json`

## Symptom

Every hook (SessionStart/PreToolUse/PostToolUse/Stop) fails with exit 2 — Claude Code shows a blocking hook error on every tool call and re-prompts the model on every Stop. The only stderr is:

```
/usr/bin/bash: line 1: printf: write error: Permission denied
```

That message is a **red herring** and cost us hours: it's MSYS bash mislabelling **EPIPE** as `Permission denied`. In the hook command, the candidate-path group keeps writing after the `while IFS= read … break` reader has closed the pipe (reliably triggered when `CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT` is set, since the first candidate matches immediately). Cosmetic — but it masks the real failure below.

## Actual root cause (worker down, cannot self-heal)

`~/.claude-mem/logs/claude-mem-<date>.log` showed the real chain:

```
[SYSTEM] Worker PID file points to a live process, skipping duplicate spawn
[SYSTEM] Live PID detected but worker did not become healthy before timeout — likely still starting
[SYSTEM] Cannot lazy-spawn worker: Bun runtime not found on PATH
```

1. `~/.claude-mem/worker.pid` was **stale** — in our case the recorded PID (8096) no longer existed at all (`Get-Process -Id 8096` → nothing), yet the liveness check reported "live process", so no worker was spawned. Nothing was listening on the worker port (37777 on Windows), and every hook call exited 2 (`BLOCKING_ERROR`).
2. The **lazy-spawn fallback couldn't recover** because it only looks for Bun on `PATH`, and the hooks' PATH bootstrap (`$SHELL -lc 'echo $PATH'`) doesn't include `~/.bun/bin` on a stock Git Bash setup.

Manual fix that worked: delete `worker.pid`, run `worker-service.cjs start` with `~/.bun/bin` prepended to PATH → worker healthy, all hooks exit 0.

## Suggested fixes

1. **PID staleness:** treat port health as the source of truth — if the health check times out, clear/ignore the PID file and spawn, instead of trusting PID liveness (PID reuse also makes the check unreliable even when a process *does* exist). 
2. **Lazy-spawn:** probe well-known Bun locations (`~/.bun/bin/bun`, `BUN_INSTALL`) in addition to PATH.
3. **Hook noise:** silence the candidate group's stderr (e.g. `{ …; } 2>/dev/null | while IFS= read …`) or restructure so nothing writes after the reader breaks — on MSYS the EPIPE surfaces as a confusing `Permission denied` that Claude Code presents as the hook error, hiding the real cause.
4. Consider degrading gracefully (exit 0 with a warning) when the worker is unreachable, so a down worker doesn't block every tool call in the host session.

Happy to provide fuller logs or test a patch. Thanks for the plugin!

title: chroma-mcp orphan leak still present on 13.10.3 — OOMs an 8GB machine in ~20 min (regression of #1063 / #1077)
author: mglmnz
body:
Orphaned `chroma-mcp` processes still accumulate until the machine is out of memory, on **13.10.3**.

This looks like a regression (or an incomplete fix) of #1063 (closed via #1065) and #1077 (closed). The changelog for **v13.0.1** states *"enforce a single `chroma-mcp` subprocess per worker (singleton via `disposeCurrentSubprocess()` on every code path; tree-kill of orphans on dispose)"* — but the leak reproduces on 13.10.3, several minors later.

### Environment

- claude-mem **13.10.3** (plugin, `thedotmack` marketplace)
- Fedora Asahi Remix 43, aarch64 (Apple M2), **8 GB RAM**, 16 KB pages
- Backend: Chroma, `CLAUDE_MEM_DATA_DIR=~/.claude-mem`, index ~1.7 GB
- Typically 2–3 concurrent Claude Code sessions, left running for days

### Symptom

Hard system freeze every ~20–30 min under load; only a force power-cycle recovers. Root cause turned out to be orphaned `chroma-mcp` processes exhausting RAM and swap.

### Measurements

Orphan accumulation with 2 sessions active:

```
54 chroma-mcp procs,  998 MB RSS   (26 min uptime)
44 chroma-mcp procs,  ~2 GB RSS    (25 min after a manual sweep)
```

State immediately before intervention:

```
Mem:  7.3Gi total, 6.9Gi used, 389Mi available
Swap:  15Gi total,  12Gi used
```

Killing **only the orphans** (nothing else changed) immediately recovered 8.5 GB of swap:

| | before | after |
|---|---|---|
| RAM available | 389 MB | 2.5 GB |
| Swap used | 12 GB | 3.5 GB |
| chroma-mcp procs | 57 | 4 |

### Spawn characteristics

- A new orphan pair (`uv` wrapper + its python child) appears roughly **every 20–60 s** while a session is active.
- **The trigger is tool-call activity, not a timer.** Controlled test — idle 120 s with zero tool calls:

  ```
  t=0    chroma procs: 18
  t=60   chroma procs: 18
  t=120  chroma procs: 18   -> flat while idle
  ```

  The count only grows while tool calls are being made. That is consistent with the PostToolUse → `syncObservation()` → `ensureConnection()` path described in #1063.

- Orphans are reparented to `systemd --user`, whereas a live one is a child of the running `mcp-server.cjs`. This makes them trivially separable:

  ```
  ORPHAN  pid=88967  ppid=3069(systemd)  age=1136s
  ORPHAN  pid=91217  ppid=3069(systemd)  age=1094s
  ORPHAN  pid=94987  ppid=3069(systemd)  age=1014s
  ...
  ```

- Most orphans stall at 2–4 MB RSS (they appear to hang before fully initialising), but a subset load the full index and sit at **100–260 MB each**. Those are what exhaust memory.

### Note on impact

On a 32 GB machine this presents slowly, as general "the system got sluggish." On 8 GB it is fatal within ~20 minutes of active use and presents as an unexplained hard freeze — I chased disk space and swap configuration for a while before finding the process swarm. Worth considering a guard regardless of the root-cause fix.

### Workaround currently in use

A `systemd --user` timer sweeping every 3 minutes, killing only `chroma-mcp` whose parent is `pid 1` or `systemd --user` (i.e. definitively not attached to any live `mcp-server.cjs`), with a 120 s minimum age to avoid racing a fresh spawn. This keeps the count near zero and never touches the index or stored memories.

Happy to share the script, or to test a patch on this machine — the leak reproduces here within minutes, so it's an easy environment to verify a fix against.

title: Observations inherit the session's launch-time project, not the per-prompt derived one (silently misattributes memories across repos/worktrees)
author: decleezy
body:
## Summary

`sdk_sessions.project` is bound **once, at session creation**, from the launch cwd, and every observation inherits it. But claude-mem already re-derives the correct (worktree-aware) project on **every prompt** and logs it as `INIT_COMPLETE` — it just discards that value at write time.

The result: any session that outlives its launch directory files all of its memories under the wrong project. On my machine this silently misattributed **71% of the last three months of observations**, and the affected project's memory bucket appeared frozen for 12 weeks while the plugin looked perfectly healthy.

## Evidence

The read path resolves correctly, per prompt (`~/.claude-mem/logs/`):

```
[session-233] INIT_COMPLETE | promptNumber=100 | project=dummy-project/feature-1
[session-233] INIT_COMPLETE | promptNumber=101 | project=dummy-project
[session-233] INIT_COMPLETE | promptNumber=102 | project=dummy-project/feature-3
```

The write path ignores all of it. For that same session:

```sql
sqlite> SELECT o.project, COUNT(*) FROM observations o
        JOIN sdk_sessions s ON s.memory_session_id = o.memory_session_id
        WHERE s.id = 233 GROUP BY 1;

dummy-project-related | 1173      -- the launch cwd, from 10 days earlier
```

Corroborating: **zero** observations exist under any worktree-style name despite ~120 prompts deriving them, and `merged_into_project` is NULL across all 7,617 rows, so the worktree roll-up path never fires either.

```sql
sqlite> SELECT COUNT(*) FROM observations WHERE project LIKE '%/%';   -- 0
```

## Reproduce

1. `cd repo-a && claude` — session row is stamped `project=repo-a`.
2. In that same session, work in `repo-b` (or a git worktree of `repo-a`).
3. Watch the logs: `INIT_COMPLETE` correctly reports `project=repo-b` / `repo-a/worktree`.
4. Query the DB: every observation is still stamped `repo-a`.

Two workflows hit this constantly:
- **Long-lived sessions** that move across repos (mine ran 10 days).
- **git worktrees** — `Sr()` computes the nice `parent/worktree` name and nothing ever persists it.

A session launched from a non-git scratch dir is the worst case: mine was launched from `~/code/tmp/random`, so months of real project work landed in a bucket literally named `random`.

## Suggested fix

The correct value is already in hand at write time — `INIT_COMPLETE` proves it. Either:

1. **Stamp observations with the per-prompt derived project** rather than inheriting `sdk_sessions.project` (observations already carry `prompt_number`, so the association is clean); or
2. **Update `sdk_sessions.project` when the derived project changes**, at minimum so new observations follow the session's actual location.

(1) is strictly better — it keeps a multi-repo session's memories attributed to the repo each one actually came from, which (2) cannot do.

## Recovery, for anyone else who hits this

The logs preserve exactly what the writer dropped. Since `observations.prompt_number` is populated and `INIT_COMPLETE` carries `sessionDbId` + `promptNumber` + the correct project, historical rows can be relabeled by joining the two — no heuristics needed. I recovered 1,708 observations this way (plus the mirrored `project` metadata in `chroma.sqlite3`, which goes stale the same way and must be synced separately, keyed on `sqlite_id` / `doc_type='observation'`).

**Version:** 13.10.3
**Platform:** macOS (darwin 25.5.0), Claude Code

title: Stale-version recycle loop leaks chroma-mcp processes (759 procs / ~71 GB RSS) until the machine OOMs
author: jhh3
body:
## Summary

After a plugin auto-update created a hooks/worker version skew (`pluginVersion=13.10.3` hooks vs a running worker reporting `13.10.2`), the stale-version recycle path looped for ~2 hours, spawning a fresh `uvx` + `chroma-mcp` process pair roughly every 14–24 seconds and orphaning the previous pair each time. Result: **759 leaked `chroma-mcp` processes totaling ~71 GB RSS**, which drove the machine (128 GB RAM, no swap) into OOM — systemd-oomd and the kernel OOM killer killed Chrome (twice), Spotify, and Slack before the box recovered.

## Environment

- claude-mem `13.10.3` (community-edge), installed via the `thedotmack` marketplace, user scope
- Claude Code CLI 2.1.207, Bun runtime
- Ubuntu (kernel 7.0.11), 128 GB RAM, no swap
- Several long-running Claude Code sessions (each generating hook events → each hook event is a recycle attempt)

## Timeline (from `~/.claude-mem/logs/claude-mem-2026-07-12.log`)

- **14:18:36** — plugin auto-update lands; first `Worker version mismatch — recycling stale worker {pluginVersion=13.10.3, workerVersion=13.10.2}`
- **14:18 → 16:19** — the mismatch line repeats **882×**; worker background init runs **447×**; each init spawns a chroma pair; prior pairs orphan to PID 1
- **16:19:14** — last mismatch; a 13.10.3 worker finally claims the port and the loop ends (in our case only because the stuck worker's chroma transport was externally killed)

## Log evidence (counts over the 2-hour window)

```
882  Worker version mismatch — recycling stale worker {pluginVersion=13.10.3, workerVersion=13.10.2}
447  [WORKER] Background initialization starting...
446  [SYSTEM] Shutdown initiated
446  [ERROR] [SYSTEM] Graceful shutdown failed — proceeding {reason=restart} Server is not running.
439  [SYSTEM] Worker is ready but still reports a stale version; not recycling again in this hook invocation (one recycle per hook event)
431  [SYSTEM] Port already in use, refusing to start duplicate
449  [CHROMA_MCP] Prewarming chroma-mcp uvx environment
398  [CHROMA_MCP] Connecting to chroma-mcp via MCP stdio
379  [CHROMA_MCP] Connected to chroma-mcp successfully
```

Process-table state at the end: 759 processes matching `chroma-mcp` (mix of `uv tool uvx ... chroma-mcp` wrappers and their python children), 378 of them reparented to PID 1, ~71 GB combined RSS. 742 of the 759 were spawned that day, at a steady ~250/hour.

## Root cause chain (correlated with `src/` on main)

1. **Doomed duplicate workers leak the chroma pair they just spawned.** During the loop, replacement workers run background initialization — which prewarms/spawns the `uvx` + `chroma-mcp` pair — and only then lose the port race (`Port already in use, refusing to start duplicate`, 431×) and exit without killing the children they spawned. This is the direct source of the leak (447 inits ≈ 742 leaked processes). The #2313 singleton tree-kill in `ChromaMcpManager` doesn't help here because the orphans belong to *dead* worker processes — nothing sweeps chroma processes left behind by a previous worker incarnation.
2. **Graceful shutdown failed on every cycle with `Server is not running.`** (446×), so whatever cleanup lives in `performGracefulShutdown` never ran, and `runShutdownSequence` (`src/services/worker-shutdown.ts`) has no last-resort child tree-kill on the non-graceful path — it logs and proceeds.
3. **The restart handoff resurrects the stale version.** The dying worker spawns its successor via `resolveSuccessorScript()` from its own install path, so the 13.10.2 worker kept re-spawning a 13.10.2 successor while 13.10.3 hooks kept demanding a recycle. The skew therefore never self-heals; it persisted until the stale worker was killed externally.
4. **No global backoff on recycles.** "One recycle per hook event" caps each event, but with several active sessions that still meant a recycle attempt every ~14–24s for two hours. A persistent failure marker (e.g. "recycle to 13.10.3 has failed N times recently — stop trying and surface an error") would have capped the damage.

## Suggested fixes (roughly in impact order)

1. On the "refusing to start duplicate" abort path, tree-kill any children the aborting worker already spawned (chroma pair, prewarm processes) before exiting — or defer chroma spawn/prewarm until after the port is won.
2. On worker startup, sweep and kill leftover `chroma-mcp` processes recorded by previous incarnations (the supervisor's process registry already persists PIDs to `supervisor.json`) or any `chroma-mcp --data-dir <this data dir>` process not owned by a live registered worker.
3. Make the restart handoff resolve the successor script from the *currently installed* plugin version rather than the dying worker's own path, so a version-skew recycle actually converges.
4. Fix the `Server is not running.` throw in graceful shutdown (state tracking seems wrong when the HTTP server never fully started / already closed), and add a best-effort child tree-kill to the failure path of `runShutdownSequence`.
5. Add cross-event backoff for stale-version recycles.

Happy to provide fuller log excerpts if useful. Thanks for claude-mem!

title: Per-event hook path costs ~1s per tool call (2.4MB bundle re-loaded each time)
author: NiceLeader
body:
**Version:** 13.10.2, Windows 11, Node 22.18

## Measurements

Real hook path, correct args, real payload (exit 0, returns `{}`):

```
echo '{"session_id":"t","tool_name":"Read","tool_input":{"file_path":"x"},"cwd":"D:/x"}' \
  | node scripts/bun-runner.js scripts/worker-service.cjs hook claude-code observation
```

3-run timings: **1178 / 1049 / 1047 ms** (median ~1050 ms).

Bootstrap floor, measured separately (deliberately wrong args so the process
only spawns, loads the bundle and prints usage): **373–379 ms**, of which:

| component | cost |
|---|---|
| bare `node -e ""` startup (this machine) | 67 ms |
| `bun-runner.js` resolution | +7 ms |
| second process spawn for worker-service | ~70 ms |
| `require()` of `worker-service.cjs` (2,459,640 bytes) | ~132 ms |

So every hook event pays ~370 ms just to bootstrap before any real work, and
the full observation path lands at ~1 s. With hooks registered on
PostToolUse/UserPromptSubmit/SessionStart/Stop, a 500-tool-call session
accumulates on the order of **8+ minutes** of added latency on this machine.
The hooks.json wrapper (PATH resolution / plugin-root scan) adds more on top
in real sessions.

## Root cause (bootstrap part)

The hook client and the worker service are the same 2.4MB bundle. Each hook
event spawns a fresh process that parses/initializes the entire bundle just to
hand one small JSON payload onward.

## Suggested fix

Split a **thin hook client** out of the bundle: a dependency-free ~20-line
script that reads stdin, resolves the worker endpoint, hands off the payload,
and exits — capping per-event cost near bare node startup (~70-80 ms here).
If the observation path also does synchronous heavy work in the child (the
remaining ~700 ms), moving it behind the handoff would compound the win.

Happy to test a patch on this setup (same machine as my report in #997).

title: Auth-failure prose ("Failed to authenticate. API Error: 401") still drops the claimed batch — only quota-limit prose is preserved for retry
author: bencorfai
body:
## Summary

When the Claude provider's generator turn returns an **auth-failure text response** (e.g. `Failed to authenticate. API Error: 401 · Please run /login`), the worker classifies it as non-XML prose, logs that the batch is being ignored, and **confirms the claimed messages** — permanently deleting them from `pending_messages`. Since auth failures are transient (a `/login` fixes them), every observation queued during the auth outage is silently and unrecoverably lost.

#1874 fixed the general "unconditional confirm on non-XML output" problem, and current code preserves the batch for **quota/usage-limit** prose — but auth-failure prose still falls through to the drop path.

## Where it happens (verified against the v13.10.2 shipped bundle, `plugin/scripts/worker-service.cjs`)

In the response-processing path, when the parse is invalid (minified names from the bundle, so approximate):

- If the prose matches the **usage-limit detector** (`PW(t)` — regexes for "usage limit reached", "weekly quota exceeded", etc.): the worker logs `returned quota-limit prose — pausing generator and preserving queued batch`, calls `resetProcessingToPending(...)`, and aborts the generator. ✅ Messages survive and are retried.
- **Any other prose** — including `Failed to authenticate. API Error: 401` — hits the else branch: `returned non-XML prose response — ignoring queued batch`, increments `consecutiveInvalidOutputs`, and calls `confirmClaimedMessages(...)`. ❌ The batch is deleted even though nothing was stored.

There is no auth-failure detector equivalent to the quota one, so an expired/broken CLI login turns into 100% silent observation loss until someone notices (and the drop is only visible at WARN/DEBUG log level).

## Observed live

On v12.1.3 (macOS, bun worker, `CLAUDE_MEM_PROVIDER=claude` with CLI OAuth): the CLI's OAuth token was invalid, every generator turn returned the 401 text, the worker logged `non-XML response; observation content was discarded` (the v12 wording), and each batch was deleted from `pending_messages`. Nothing distinguished "model produced garbage" from "provider is down"; the queue drained to nowhere for days.

I then confirmed by inspection of the v13.10.2 bundle that the behavior for auth prose is unchanged (only the quota case was carved out).

## Suggested fix

Treat auth-failure prose the same way as quota-limit prose:

- Add an auth detector alongside the quota detector, e.g. `/failed to authenticate|api error:?\s*(401|403)|please run \/login/i`.
- On match: preserve the batch (`resetProcessingToPending`), pause/abort the generator, and surface a visible "provider auth is broken — run /login" health signal instead of counting it toward `consecutiveInvalidOutputs`.
- Alternatively (more general): never `confirmClaimedMessages` when zero observations were parsed — mark the messages failed with a `retry_count` so a later healthy session can pick them up, and only hard-drop after N failed retries.

## Related issues

- #1874 — the original "silently deletes queued messages on non-XML output" bug (closed via PR #2079; that fix became the quota-only preservation path).
- #3105 — open; about the 403 auth failure itself on clean installs. This issue is complementary: whatever the auth root cause, the queue shouldn't destroy data while auth is broken.
- #3193 — open; empty-batch prose churning the 3-strikes counter. Same classification machinery, different failure mode.

## Environment

- claude-mem 12.1.3 (live repro) / v13.10.2 (code inspection)
- macOS (darwin 25.5.0), bun worker
- Claude Code CLI 2.1.159
- `CLAUDE_MEM_PROVIDER=claude`, CLI OAuth auth

title: PostToolUse observation hook blocks main loop for ~2.4s per tool call — should be async
author: 888dk666
body:
## Environment
- claude-mem: 13.2.0 (verified same hook config on main branch as of 2026-07-12)
- Claude Code: 2.1.207
- Install: npm global via nvm (node v24.15.0)
- OS: macOS 26.5

## Problem

Every PostToolUse observation hook invocation blocks the Claude Code main loop for **~2.4 seconds** because none of the hooks in `plugin/hooks/hooks.json` are marked `"async": true`.

## Hard data (from 20 recent session transcripts, ~25,000 hook entries)

| Hook | Count | Avg | P50 | P95 | Max | Total time wasted |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| PostToolUse:Bash (observation) | 2,483 | 2,418ms | 2,099ms | 3,688ms | 94,891ms | ~100 minutes |
| PostToolUse:Read (observation) | 832 | 2,280ms | 2,060ms | 3,533ms | 6,421ms | ~32 minutes |
| PreToolUse:Read (file-context) | 626 | 2,189ms | 2,047ms | 2,955ms | 15,267ms | ~23 minutes |
| Stop (summarize) | 492 | 2,385ms | 2,155ms | 4,001ms | 8,700ms | ~20 minutes |
| PostToolUse:Edit (observation) | 344 | 2,357ms | 2,158ms | 3,488ms | 10,222ms | ~14 minutes |

**~175 minutes cumulative blocking time** in a ~48-hour observation window. The matcher `"*"` on PostToolUse means this fires on EVERY tool call — Bash, Read, Edit, Write, WebSearch, WebFetch, Skill, Agent, Workflow, everything.

## Root cause

`plugin/hooks/hooks.json` defines PostToolUse with `"matcher": "*"` and no `"async": true`:

```json
{
  "matcher": "*",
  "hooks": [{
    "type": "command",
    "command": "export PATH=...; node .../bun-runner.js .../worker-service.cjs hook claude-code observation",
    "timeout": 120
  }]
}
```

Observation recording is fire-and-forget logging — it should never block the tool loop. The fix is one line:

```json
"async": true
```

## Additional concern

On the main branch, `hooks.json` now includes a `PreToolUse matcher=Read` entry (the file-context hook previously only in `codex-hooks.json`). This means upgrading from 13.2.0 to current will add ANOTHER blocking hook before every Read — making the problem worse, not better.

## Expected behavior

All observation/logging hooks (`PostToolUse observation`, `PreToolUse file-context`, `Stop summarize`) should run with `"async": true` so they don't block the Claude Code tool loop. The user should never wait 2+ seconds after every tool call for background logging to complete.

## Proposed fix

Add `"async": true` to these hooks in `plugin/hooks/hooks.json`:
- `PostToolUse` matcher `*` → observation
- `PreToolUse` matcher `Read` → file-context
- `Stop` → summarize

Optionally reduce the `PostToolUse` matcher from `*` to `Read|Write|Edit|Bash` since observation on `WebSearch`/`WebFetch`/`Skill`/`Agent` adds latency without meaningful observation data.

## Claude Code reference

Per Claude Code hooks documentation, `"async": true` runs the hook in the background without blocking: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks

title: chroma-mcp process leak: stale marketplace version triggers an unbounded worker-recycle loop, and recycled workers orphan chroma-mcp to PID 1
author: rohithhn
body:
## Summary

On a machine with **more than one `CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR`** (or any config dir whose
marketplace checkout lags the installed worker build), claude-mem leaks
`uvx … chroma-mcp --client-type persistent` process trees at a rate of roughly
**one every 15–30 s** during active work. Each orphan holds **60–330 MB RSS**
(RES grows the longer it lives; observed up to ~580 MB). They reparent to PID 1
and never exit on their own.

There are **two independent defects** stacked on top of each other:

1. **Trigger — permanent worker-recycle loop on a version mismatch that can
   never resolve.** `wv()` recycles the worker whenever the *plugin* version
   (read from the marketplace checkout's `package.json`) ≠ the *worker's*
   baked-in build version, then waits for a successor **reporting the plugin
   version** — which a freshly spawned worker can never report. So the wait
   always times out, it lazy-spawns anyway, and the next hook repeats it. Result:
   the shared worker daemon is killed and respawned on essentially every hook.

2. **Leak mechanism — the `chroma-mcp` child is only killed on the graceful
   shutdown path.** The child is spawned non-detached by the MCP
   `StdioClientTransport`. On every non-graceful exit (SIGKILL after a restart
   timeout, the `EADDRINUSE` duplicate-daemon `process.exit(0)`, or a crash) the
   `uv → python chroma-mcp` tree is not killed and reparents to PID 1.

Either defect alone is survivable; together they leak continuously.

## Environment

- claude-mem `v13.10.2` (Claude Code plugin, `thedotmack/claude-mem`)
- macOS (Darwin 25.5.0), Apple Silicon
- Bun runner; `uv`/`uvx` at `~/.local/bin`; Python 3.13 (Homebrew)
- chroma launched as: `uvx --python 3.13 --with onnxruntime>=1.20 --with protobuf<7 --from chroma-mcp==0.2.6 chroma-mcp --client-type persistent --data-dir ~/.claude-mem/chroma`
- Three config dirs in use: `~/.claude`, `~/.claude-personal` (both marketplace
  checkout at an old commit reporting **`13.4.0`**), and `~/.claude-work`
  (marketplace up to date at **`13.10.2`**). All three share one worker on port
  `37701`.

## Reproduction

1. Install claude-mem 13.10.x so the cache + worker bundle report `13.10.2`.
2. Arrange for at least one `CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR` whose
   `plugins/marketplaces/thedotmack/package.json` `version` is **older** than the
   worker build (e.g. a stale marketplace checkout, or a second profile that was
   never re-synced). Here `~/.claude` / `~/.claude-personal` were pinned at
   `13.4.0`.
3. Run any Claude Code session under that config dir and do normal work
   (tool calls → PostToolUse hooks, etc.).
4. Watch `~/.claude-mem/logs/claude-mem-YYYY-MM-DD.log`: you get a steady stream
   of

   ```
   [SYSTEM] Worker version mismatch — recycling stale worker {pluginVersion=13.4.0, workerVersion=13.10.2}
   [SYSTEM] No successor worker appeared after recycle; falling through to lazy-spawn {pluginVersion=13.4.0, workerVersion=13.10.2}
   [SYSTEM] Worker not running — lazy-spawning {…}
   [SYSTEM] Worker is ready but still reports a stale version; not recycling again in this hook invocation {pluginVersion=13.4.0, workerVersion=13.10.2}
   ```

   (86–100 `recycling stale worker` events in a single day's log in our case.)
5. `ps -eo pid,ppid,etime,rss,command | grep '[c]hroma-mcp' | awk '$2==1'` shows
   a growing set of PID-1-owned chroma trees whose `etime` is much larger than
   the current worker's age — i.e. each was spawned by a prior, now-dead worker.

## Root cause (with code references — offsets are into the minified
`scripts/worker-service.cjs` of 13.10.2)

### Defect 1 — recycle loop

- `jle()` reads the **plugin** version from the marketplace checkout:
  `Vs = join(CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR, "plugins","marketplaces","thedotmack")`, then
  `JSON.parse(readFileSync(join(Vs,"package.json"))).version`.
  (`Vs` def near offset 190065; `jle` near 232303.)
- `mU(port)` returns `matches: pluginVersion === workerVersion`
  (`workerVersion` = worker `/api/health` `.version`, i.e. the bundle's own
  version). (near 232806.)
- `wv()` (near 239058) — invoked by `Jle()` (near 241107), which is the
  reachability gate at the top of **`dn()`**, the HTTP helper every hook uses
  (near 242731). So `wv()` runs on essentially every hook. On mismatch:

  ```
  h.info("SYSTEM","Worker version mismatch — recycling stale worker",{pluginVersion:o,workerVersion:a});
  await In("/api/admin/restart", {method:"POST", …});           // kill current worker
  if (await Yle(o)) { … }                                        // WAIT FOR SUCCESSOR REPORTING plugin version `o`
  else h.warn("SYSTEM","No successor worker appeared after recycle; falling through to lazy-spawn", …);
  // lazy-spawn a fresh --daemon (detached:true)
  ```

- `Yle(t)` (near 234-…, "Worker health-version fetch") polls until a worker's
  `/api/health` version === `t` (the **plugin** version). A freshly spawned
  worker always reports **its own bundle version** (13.10.2), never the stale
  plugin version (13.4.0), so `Yle` **always times out** → lazy-spawn → next
  hook mismatches again → unbounded recycle loop.

The bug is that the successor-readiness check keys on the *plugin manifest*
version, which a newly spawned worker cannot satisfy when the manifest is stale.
There is no semver "is plugin newer than worker?" gate and no circuit breaker, so
a stale/older manifest loops forever instead of no-op'ing.

### Defect 2 — chroma child leaks on non-graceful exit

- The chroma child is spawned by the MCP `StdioClientTransport` (`ra.start`,
  near 184746):

  ```
  this._process = spawn(command, args, {
    env:{…}, stdio:["pipe","pipe", serverParams.stderr ?? "inherit"],
    shell:false, windowsHide:…, cwd
  });
  ```

  No `detached`, so the child shares the worker's process group but is **not**
  auto-killed when the worker dies; on parent death `uv` reparents to PID 1 and
  keeps its `python chroma-mcp` grandchild alive.

- The only teardown is the **graceful** path `hU()` (near 232-…):
  `… mcpClient.close() … chromaMcpManager.stop() …` where
  `ChromaMcpClient.stop()/disposeCurrentSubprocess()` calls `killProcessTree`
  (recursive `pgrep -P`, near 1245183). This runs only on clean
  SIGTERM/SIGINT/shutdown.

- Non-graceful exits that skip `hU()` and therefore leak:
  - restart via `/api/admin/restart` that ends in a SIGKILL after the graceful
    timeout;
  - **duplicate-daemon race**: `new g0().start().catch(… EADDRINUSE … process.exit(0))`
    (near 2452616) — a daemon that loses the port race calls `process.exit(0)`
    directly, with no chroma cleanup, after it may already have begun a chroma
    connection;
  - any uncaught crash.

## Observed impact

- Leak rate ≈ 1 chroma tree / 15–30 s of active work (tracks the per-hook
  recycle rate × number of concurrent config dirs/sessions).
- 60–330 MB RSS per orphan (observed one at ~580 MB).
- The worker PID changed 4× in ~4 minutes while observing (14072 → 15973 →
  16267 → 18580 → 19144), confirming the daemon is flapping, not stable.
- Users currently have to run an external watchdog (a launchd job reaping
  PID-1-owned chroma trees older than N seconds) to keep memory bounded.

## Suggested fixes

### Fix A (stops the trigger — recommended, small)

In `wv()`/`mU()`:
- Only recycle when the plugin version is **strictly newer** than the worker
  version (semver compare), never when it is equal or older. A stale/older
  marketplace manifest should be a no-op, not a recycle.
- Make the successor check (`Yle`) wait for **readiness**, not for the worker to
  report the *plugin manifest* version (which a fresh worker can't produce when
  the manifest is stale). Compare against the version a freshly spawned worker
  will actually report, or just require readiness + `matches` becoming true.
- Add a recycle circuit breaker (e.g. max N recycles / M minutes per host) so no
  mismatch can loop unboundedly.

Config workaround (what we applied on the affected machine): correct the stale
`version` in each stale `CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR/plugins/marketplaces/thedotmack/package.json`
to match the installed build (`13.4.0` → `13.10.2`). This is the value `jle()`
reads; once it matches the worker build, `wv()` stops recycling. (Equivalently:
`git pull` / re-sync the marketplace checkout.)

### Fix B (defense in depth — kill chroma on all exit paths)

- Spawn the chroma child **detached** (its own process group) and register, once,
  handlers that synchronously kill the whole group on any worker exit:

  ```js
  const child = spawn(cmd, args, { detached: true, stdio: […] });
  const killGroup = () => { try { process.kill(-child.pid, "SIGKILL"); } catch {} };
  process.once("exit", killGroup);
  process.once("SIGTERM", () => { killGroup(); process.exit(0); });
  process.once("SIGINT",  () => { killGroup(); process.exit(0); });
  process.once("uncaughtException", (e) => { killGroup(); throw e; });
  ```

  (macOS has no `PR_SET_PDEATHSIG`; a detached group + an `exit` handler is the
  portable way to guarantee the `uv → python` tree dies with the worker.)
- Run chroma cleanup before the `EADDRINUSE` duplicate-daemon `process.exit(0)`
  (near 2452616) and on the restart/SIGKILL path.

Fix A removes the storm; Fix B ensures that even legitimate restarts/crashes
never orphan chroma.

title: Windows: worker restart leaves orphaned LISTENING socket, permanently blocking the worker port — every hook then hangs 60–120s
author: thiagofstp
body:
## Summary

On Windows, the worker restart flow can kill the worker without a graceful shutdown and fail to spawn its successor. The dead process's socket stays in the TCP table as `LISTENING` (owned by a PID that no longer exists), permanently blocking the configured worker port until reboot. From then on, **every** hook invocation (`UserPromptSubmit`, `PostToolUse`, `PreToolUse` Read) hangs for its full 60–120s timeout on every prompt and tool call, with zero tokens consumed — Claude Code sessions feel frozen.

This happened **twice in two days** on the same machine: port `37778` on 2026-07-11 and port `37779` on 2026-07-12. Each time the only recovery was manually changing `CLAUDE_MEM_WORKER_PORT` in `~/.claude-mem/settings.json` (or rebooting Windows to clear the orphaned socket).

## Environment

- **Claude-mem**: 13.10.2
- **Claude Code**: 2.1.207
- **Node.js**: v24.3.0
- **Bun**: 1.3.13
- **OS**: Microsoft Windows 11 Home 10.0.26200 (x64)
- **Worker**: Not running (port orphaned at time of report)
- **Key settings**: `CLAUDE_MEM_WORKER_PORT: 37780` (already bumped twice as workaround: 37778 → 37779 → 37780)

## Failure sequence (from logs)

1. Worker running fine on port 37779.
2. Plugin triggers an internal restart (`reason=restart`, apparently after a version check).
3. Graceful shutdown fails: `Graceful shutdown failed — proceeding {reason=restart} Server is not running.`
4. Restart successor fails to spawn (`pid=0`).
5. The dead PID keeps the `LISTENING` socket bound in the Windows TCP table.
6. Every new worker start attempt fails with `Is port 37779 in use?`, and every hook client connects to the dead listener's kernel backlog and waits forever — burning the entire hook timeout (60–120s) instead of failing fast.

## Log evidence (`claude-mem-2026-07-12.log`)

```
[2026-07-11 23:52:10.457] [INFO ] [SYSTEM] HTTP server started {host=127.0.0.1, port=37779, pid=26440}
[2026-07-11 23:52:11.304] [INFO ] [SYSTEM] Worker started {host=127.0.0.1, port=37779, pid=26440}
[2026-07-11 23:52:14.175] [ERROR] [SYSTEM] Graceful shutdown failed — proceeding {reason=restart} Server is not running.
[2026-07-11 23:53:11.583] [WARN ] [SYSTEM] Worker port did not open after lazy-spawn within the cold-boot wait (~15s)
[2026-07-11 23:57:11.880] [INFO ] [SYSTEM] Restart successor spawned {pid=0, script=...\worker-service.cjs, port=37779}
[2026-07-12 00:02:30.885] [ERROR] [SYSTEM] ✗ Worker failed to start Failed to start server. Is port 37779 in use?
[2026-07-12 00:07:13.654] [ERROR] [SYSTEM] ✗ Worker failed to start Failed to start server. Is port 37779 in use?
```

## OS-level evidence

```
> netstat -ano | findstr :37779
  TCP    127.0.0.1:37779        0.0.0.0:0              LISTENING       26440
  TCP    127.0.0.1:37779        127.0.0.1:57441        ESTABLISHED     26440
  TCP    127.0.0.1:57441        127.0.0.1:37779        ESTABLISHED     2056

> tasklist /FI "PID eq 26440"
INFO: No tasks are running which match the specified criteria.
```

PID 26440 (the dead worker) still owns the `LISTENING` socket. PID 2056 was a live `bun.exe` hook client stuck connected to the dead listener, waiting until the hook timeout.

## Expected behavior

- Worker restart should shut down the old server gracefully **before** spawning the successor, and verify the successor actually bound the port.
- On startup, if the configured port is held by a PID that no longer exists, the worker should detect this and fall back to another port automatically instead of failing forever.
- Hook clients should use a short connect/response timeout and fail fast when the worker is unreachable, so a broken worker degrades silently instead of freezing every Claude Code interaction for 60–120s.

## Steps to reproduce

1. On Windows, run claude-mem v13.10.2 with the worker on its configured port.
2. Trigger the internal worker restart flow (observed with `reason=restart` after a version check).
3. Observe `Graceful shutdown failed — proceeding` followed by successor spawn with `pid=0`.
4. Check `netstat -ano`: the port is still `LISTENING`, owned by a PID that no longer exists in `tasklist`.
5. Every subsequent hook call hangs 60–120s; worker start attempts fail with `Is port ... in use?`.

## Workaround

Change `CLAUDE_MEM_WORKER_PORT` in `~/.claude-mem/settings.json` to a free port, or reboot Windows to clear the orphaned socket. (Not sustainable — each failed restart can burn another port.)

title: Corrupted HNSW segment turns ChromaSync into an unbounded retry loop — multi-GB memory spikes every few minutes and frozen watermarks
author: eralpozcan
body:
## Summary

When Chroma's HNSW segment writer enters a persistent failed state, every `chroma_add_documents` call fails with the same deterministic error, ChromaSync's watermark never advances, and the sync layer retries the same batches indefinitely. Each attempt makes the chroma-mcp python subprocess replay its write-ahead log into the HNSW segment in memory, allocating gigabytes that are never returned to the OS. The result is a memory pump: the subprocess balloons to ~20 GB physical footprint within ~4 minutes of spawning, saturates the compressor/swap, and (with #3192) gets recycled — only for the successor to repeat the cycle immediately.

## Environment

- claude-mem: 13.10.2 (branch `fix/chroma-memory-watchdog`, PR #3192)
- chroma-mcp: 0.2.6 (pinned), persistent client on `~/.claude-mem/chroma`
- Host: macOS (Apple Silicon), 8 GB RAM

## Evidence

Every add fails with the same tool-level error (1,824 occurrences in a single day; 419 the day before, so the state predates that day's restarts):

```
[ERROR] [CHROMA_SYNC] Batch add failed — watermark will not advance for this batch, continuing with remaining batches {collection=cm__claude-mem, batchStart=0, batchSize=7} chroma-mcp tool "chroma_add_documents" returned error: Error executing tool chroma_add_documents: Failed to add documents to collection 'cm__claude-mem': Error executing plan: Error sending backfill request to compactor: Failed to apply logs to the hnsw segment writer
```

`vmmap --summary` of the python subprocess 4 minutes after spawn shows the allocations are almost entirely large-block churn from the log replay, not steady-state usage:

```
Physical footprint:         12.0G
MALLOC_LARGE                14.1G   (117 regions live)
MALLOC_LARGE (reserved)      3.5T   (2,024 regions reserved)
```

`ps` RSS reported ~1.5 GB for the same process while Activity Monitor showed 20 GB — the footprint/RSS divergence addressed in PR #3192.

## Root cause chain

1. The `cm__claude-mem` collection's HNSW segment is in a persistent failed state (plausibly produced by the dual-writer scenario in #3012 — two chroma-mcp processes were observed writing the same data dir on this host — or by an earlier OOM/kill during compaction). chroma has no self-repair for this; the error is deterministic on every write.
2. ChromaSync treats every batch failure identically ("log and continue"), so the watermark freezes and every future sync attempt re-sends the same batches — an unbounded hot retry loop with no failure classification, no backoff, and no escalation. This is the memory/CPU flavor of the "healthy worker, frozen observations" defect family in #2782.
3. chroma-mcp 0.2.6 never returns the replay allocations to the OS, so each retry ratchets the footprint up until the watchdog (#3191 / PR #3192) recycles the tree, and the cycle restarts.

## Recovery that worked (manual)

The Chroma index is fully derived from SQLite, so the recovery is mechanical and lossless:

1. Stop the worker.
2. Move `~/.claude-mem/chroma` aside.
3. Remove `chroma-sync-state.json` (stale watermarks would otherwise mark the fresh empty index as already synced — the bootstrap in `backfillAllProjects` only runs when the state file is absent).
4. Start the worker → full backfill from SQLite rebuilds the collection. Zero "Batch add failed" errors since.

## Proposed fix

A circuit breaker + automated recovery in ChromaSync, since the manual procedure above should never be required:

1. Count consecutive deterministic tool-level batch failures per collection (transport/availability errors excluded — they already have reconnect handling and must not trip the breaker).
2. After N consecutive failures (e.g. 5), open the breaker: stop sending writes (stops the memory pump immediately; data stays safely in SQLite behind the watermark).
3. Run automated recovery: delete the collection, reset the sync watermarks, and trigger the existing backfill pipeline to rebuild from SQLite.
4. If recovery fails, hold the breaker open for a cooldown before retrying, so a persistently broken Chroma degrades to "no vector search" instead of "machine unusable".

Happy to submit a PR implementing the above.

## Related

- #3191 / PR #3192 — memory watchdog (bounds the symptom; this issue removes the pump)
- #3012 — dual writers on one data dir (plausible corruption origin; PR #3192's startup sweep prevents recurrence)
- #2782 — umbrella for silent data-pipeline failures; the frozen-watermark behavior here is the same defect family
- #3178 — orphan reap at boot via supervisor registry (complementary to the process-table sweep in PR #3192)

title: onnxruntime>=1.20 pin makes semantic search unusable on macOS 12 / older Intel Macs (no override)
author: charlesC137
body:
### Environment
- macOS 12.7.6 (Monterey), **Intel x86_64**
- claude-mem 13.10.2
- uv 0.5.7

### Symptom
`search` always returns `Semantic search is offline (chroma-mcp prewarm exited with code 1)` and falls back to keyword. The worker retries the prewarm every ~13s indefinitely.

### Root cause (distinct from the known uvx-syntax issues)
This is **not** the bare-positional `uvx <pkg>==<ver>` syntax bug (#2879, #2939) — 13.x correctly uses `--from`. The failure is a **platform-tag mismatch**: the Chroma prewarm pins `onnxruntime>=1.20` (`worker-service.cjs`: `Tue=["onnxruntime>=1.20","protobuf<7"]`), and onnxruntime dropped macOS-12 x86_64 wheels at 1.20 (they now require macOS 13+). uv can resolve nothing:

```
× No solution found when resolving tool dependencies:
  ╰─▶ ... onnxruntime>=1.20.0 has no wheels with a matching platform tag ...
```

`CLAUDE_MEM_PYTHON_VERSION` is overridable, but there is **no override for the onnxruntime constraint**, so no supported config can fix this on macOS < 13.

### Two secondary snags once the pin is relaxed (worth documenting)
1. `onnxruntime<1.20` has no cp313 wheel → the Python version must also drop to 3.12.
2. chroma-mcp 0.2.6's console-script launcher calls `realpath` in its shebang polyglot (`'''exec' "$(dirname -- "$(realpath -- "$0")")"/'python' ...`). macOS ships no `realpath`, so it dies with `realpath: command not found` and falls back to a bogus `$PWD/python` path.

### Suggested fix
Expose the onnxruntime constraint (ideally the whole `--with` list) via `settings.json` / env, the same way `CLAUDE_MEM_PYTHON_VERSION` already is. That lets older-macOS/Intel users set `onnxruntime<1.20` + Python 3.12 without editing the bundle. Optionally auto-detect macOS < 13 and select a compatible pin.

### Confirmed working workaround
1. `~/.claude-mem/settings.json`: `"CLAUDE_MEM_PYTHON_VERSION": "3.12"`
2. Patch the bundle: `sed -i '' 's/"onnxruntime>=1.20"/"onnxruntime<1.20"/' <ver>/scripts/worker-service.cjs` (lost on every update)
3. Add a `realpath` shim on PATH: `exec /usr/bin/python3 -c 'import os,sys;print(os.path.realpath(sys.argv[-1]))' "$@"`

After a restart, chroma-mcp's persistent server boots and answers an MCP `initialize` cleanly (`serverInfo: chroma 1.6.0`), semantic search live.

### Related
Distinct cause from #2879 / #2939 (uvx syntax) and #2897 (connect timeout). Possibly link from tracking #2961 and plan #3138.

title: Worker emits `400 Invalid signature in thinking block` retry-storm on Anthropic when transcript carries non-Anthropic (Z.AI/GLM) thinking blocks
author: mironenkophil-lgtm
body:
## Summary

claude-mem's worker generates observations against **real Anthropic** (`CLAUDE_MEM_CLAUDE_AUTH_METHOD=subscription`, the default), but sends the captured conversation transcript as message history — including `thinking` blocks whose `signature` was produced by a **different provider** (Z.AI GLM, used as the main Claude Code provider via `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL`). Anthropic validates thinking-block signatures and rejects foreign ones → `400 Invalid signature in thinking block` → SDK retries up to 11× → wasted Anthropic quota, log spam, background CPU. The main agent loop is unaffected, but on a heavy session this produced **428 such errors in a single day** (and recurs every turn a thinking-enabled assistant message is in history).

## Environment

- claude-mem: **13.10.2**
- Claude Code: **2.1.202**
- Main CC provider: Z.AI GLM (`ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL=https://api.z.ai/api/anthropic`, `ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_SONNET_MODEL=glm-5.2`)
- claude-mem auth: `CLAUDE_MEM_PROVIDER=claude`, `CLAUDE_MEM_CLAUDE_AUTH_METHOD=subscription` (defaults — worker calls real api.anthropic.com, bypassing the GLM token/`ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL`)

## Reproduction

1. Configure the CC main loop against a non-Anthropic Anthropic-compatible endpoint that returns thinking blocks (e.g. Z.AI GLM).
2. Keep claude-mem on default subscription auth, so its worker calls real api.anthropic.com for observation generation.
3. Run a multi-turn session with extended thinking enabled; let claude-mem generate observations.
4. In `~/.vscode-server/data/logs/*/Anthropic.claude-code/Claude VSCode.log`:
   ```
   [ERROR] API error (attempt 1/11): 400 400 {"type":"error","error":{"type":"invalid_request_error","message":"messages.1.content.0: Invalid `signature` in `thinking` block"},"request_id":"req_011Ccu…"}
   [WARN]  [thinking] server rejected a thinking block; stripping all thinking blocks and retrying.
   ```

## Evidence / root cause

- **Z.AI does not validate thinking signatures at all.** Direct calls to `api.z.ai/api/anthropic/v1/messages` return `200` for a thinking block with a FAKE signature, with NO signature field, and with an Anthropic-format signature — so the `400` is not coming from the main (GLM) provider.
- **All signature-400s carry Anthropic `req_…` request_ids** (Z.AI error-ids are timestamp-format, e.g. `20260712070400…`), confirming they originate at real api.anthropic.com.
- **claude-mem's worker is the only component here on the subscription/Anthropic path**, and the 400s correlate with claude-mem activity in the logs.
- In `scripts/worker-service.cjs`, captured transcript streams are reconstructed into `thinking` blocks carrying a `signature` (via `signature_delta` accumulation) and then sent as message history to the LLM. When that LLM is real Anthropic but the thinking blocks originated from GLM, the signatures are foreign → Anthropic rejects them.

## Suggested fix

Before sending captured transcript history to **any** LLM for observation generation, strip `thinking` / `redacted_thinking` blocks (or at minimum their `signature` fields) from assistant turns. Signatures are provider-specific and non-portable: Anthropic validates them, third-party endpoints ignore them — so stripping is safe for claude-mem's summarization calls (which don't rely on the user's thinking chain continuity) and eliminates the reject-retry tax everywhere. A toggle (e.g. `CLAUDE_MEM_STRIP_THINKING=1`, default on) would let users opt out.

## Related

- #2995 — disable reasoning for non-Anthropic providers (companion concern)
- #2656 — worker retries `400 model identifier invalid` (same retry-storm pattern on cross-provider model names)
- anthropics/claude-code#10627 — same root (thinking-block corruption on history re-send)

## Workaround

Until fixed: either point claude-mem at the same non-Anthropic provider (`CLAUDE_MEM_PROVIDER`/`CLAUDE_MEM_MODEL` — note claude-* model names don't resolve on Z.AI, returns `1211 Unknown Model`), or tolerate the background retry tax, which does not affect the main agent loop or generation quality.

title: [Windows] findBun() doesn't check for bun.exe via 'where bun' — falls back to bare 'bun', corrupted by shell quoting (related to #2528)
author: SiNaPsEr0x

title: [Windows] Worker never starts when %USERPROFILE% contains a space — unquoted Start-Process -ArgumentList still present in v13.10.2 (bug 1 of #2521)
author: smurfspec
body:
## Summary

On Windows, the worker daemon dies instantly at spawn when `%USERPROFILE%` contains a space (e.g. `C:\Users\Anıl pc`). This is **bug 1 of #2521** (`-ArgumentList` does not preserve spaces through to `CreateProcess`), which was consolidated into #2378 and closed as shipped in v13.4.0 — but the unquoted-argument spawn is **still present in the shipped artifacts of v13.10.2**. Verified and reproduced today on a fresh install.

## Environment

- claude-mem **v13.10.2** (fresh install via `npx claude-mem install`)
- Windows 11 Pro (10.0.26200), Windows PowerShell 5.1
- Bun 1.3.14, Node 22.20.0
- `%USERPROFILE%` = `C:\Users\Anıl pc` (contains a space; also a non-ASCII character, see notes)

## Symptom

`npx claude-mem start` returns `{"status":"ready","message":"Worker started; still warming up"}`, but no bun process ever appears and the health endpoint never comes up. `~/.claude-mem/logs/claude-mem-*.log`:

```
[INFO ] [SYSTEM] Starting worker daemon {workerScriptPath=C:\Users\Anıl pc\.claude\plugins\marketplaces\thedotmack\plugin\scripts\worker-service.cjs}
[WARN ] [SYSTEM] Worker spawned but health endpoint not responding within window — likely still starting in background
```

…then after the cooldown gate: `{"status":"error","message":"Failed to start worker"}`. No worker log is produced anywhere (the spawn uses `stdio: 'ignore'` + `-WindowStyle Hidden`, so bun's startup error is swallowed — same observability gap described in #2521).

## Root cause

`src/services/infrastructure/ProcessManager.ts`, `spawnDaemon()`, win32 branch:

```ts
const psScript = `Start-Process -FilePath '${runtimePath.replace(/'/g, "''")}' -ArgumentList @('${scriptPath.replace(/'/g, "''")}','--daemon') -WindowStyle Hidden`;
const encodedCommand = Buffer.from(psScript, 'utf16le').toString('base64');
execSync(`powershell -NoProfile -EncodedCommand ${encodedCommand}`, { stdio: 'ignore', windowsHide: true, env });
```

Windows PowerShell **5.1** (`powershell.exe`, which this invokes) joins `-ArgumentList` elements with spaces **without quoting** when building the native command line. The script path element contains a space, so bun receives it split into two argv entries.

Direct reproduction of what the spawned bun actually sees:

```
> bun.exe "C:\Users\Anıl" "pc\.claude\plugins\marketplaces\thedotmack\plugin\scripts\worker-service.cjs" --daemon
error: Module not found "C:\Users\Anıl"
(exit code 1)
```

Instant exit, nothing logged → exactly the silent-death symptom above.

The same construction is compiled into every shipped spawn site:

- `plugin/scripts/worker-service.cjs`
- `plugin/scripts/mcp-server.cjs`
- `dist/npx-cli/index.js` (the `npx claude-mem start` path)

(The worker itself is fine: running `bun plugin/scripts/worker-service.cjs` directly with a properly quoted path starts up healthy.)

## Fix (verified locally)

Embed double quotes around the script path inside the single-quoted PS string:

```ts
-ArgumentList @('"${scriptPath.replace(/'/g, "''")}"','--daemon')
```

After patching the shipped `.cjs`/`.js` files locally with exactly this change, `npx claude-mem start` works and the daemon stays up: `/api/health` → `{"status":"ok","version":"13.10.2","initialized":true,"mcpReady":true,"dependencies":{"degraded":false}}`.

(`-FilePath` is safe as-is — it takes a single string. Only the `-ArgumentList` elements need embedded quoting. PS 7.3+ changed ArgumentList quoting behavior, but the code invokes plain `powershell`, i.e. 5.1, on every stock Windows box.)

## Related observations from the same install (possibly separate issues — happy to split them out)

1. **Installer crash on non-ASCII profile paths:** `npx claude-mem install` aborts at "Copying to marketplace directory..." with `EIO, Access is denied '\\?\C:\Users\Anıl pc\.claude\plugins\marketplaces\thedotmack\.agents'`. This is Node 22's native `fs.cpSync` failing on non-ASCII Windows paths (reproducible with a bare `fs.cpSync` call; `mkdir`/`writeFile` on the same paths work). A JS fallback copy (or `fs.cp` with manual walk) would sidestep it. I could only complete the install by running the installer with `USERPROFILE`/`HOME` pointed at the 8.3 short path (`C:\Users\ANLPC~1`) and invoking `dist/npx-cli/index.js` directly via the short path.
2. **"Installing marketplace dependencies … OK" but no `node_modules`:** after the (short-path) install reported dependencies installed, the marketplace dir had no `node_modules/` at all; a manual `npm install --omit=dev --ignore-scripts --legacy-peer-deps` in `~/.claude/plugins/marketplaces/thedotmack` was needed.
3. **`claude_cli` reported as `setup_required` despite `claude.exe` being on PATH** (`C:\Users\Anıl pc\.local\bin\claude.exe`). Setting `CLAUDE_CODE_PATH` explicitly in `~/.claude-mem/settings.json` resolved it — plausibly the same space-in-path family when probing PATH.

title: Context injection resolves project as basename(cwd) outside git repos while capture attributes to the parent project — subdir launches silently get the first-run banner instead of memory
author: zoidberg-3
body:
## Environment

- claude-mem **13.10.2** (Claude Code plugin cache install), Linux
- Worker healthy (`/api/health` ok, `initialized: true`), DB intact (2200+ observations), `hook-failures.json` clean

## Symptom

A session launched from a **subdirectory of a directory that is not a git repo** gets the first-run banner ("This project has no memory yet…") instead of injected context — on session start **and** on `/compact` — even though the database holds months of history for the parent project. Capture keeps working the whole time, so nothing is lost; the read side just looks in an empty bucket. Because the failure is silent (the banner looks like a fresh install, not an error), it's easy to attribute to "memory randomly not working."

Common trigger: background/automation jobs that pin their cwd to a subfolder, or any `claude` launch from below a non-repo home/workspace directory.

## Root cause — read and write paths resolve the project name differently

**Read path (verified in 13.10.2 code):** the SessionStart context hook (`worker-service.cjs … hook claude-code context`, matcher `startup|clear|compact`) takes `cwd` from the hook's stdin payload and resolves the project as `basename(gitRoot(cwd) ?? cwd)`. Commit `daf6d9d` (#2663) made this stable across subdirs **of a git repo** via `rev-parse --show-toplevel` — but when no git root exists anywhere above cwd, the fallback is still `basename(cwd)`, so every subdirectory of a non-repo directory becomes its own brand-new project scope on read.

**Write path (verified empirically, not reverse-engineered):** observations captured from those same subdir-launched sessions are attributed to the **parent** project. Live check on this machine: sessions launched from `/home/lc/<subdir>` (home is not a git repo) write observations that land under project `lc`, and `/api/projects` returns `{"projects":["lc"]}` only — the subdir scopes the injector looks up never exist on the write side.

So for any cwd below a non-repo directory: capture writes to `lc`, inject reads from `basename(cwd)`. The two never meet.

## Repro / direct evidence

With an existing history under project `lc` (home dir `/home/lc`, not a git repo):

```
$ echo '{"session_id":"t1","hook_event_name":"SessionStart","source":"compact","cwd":"/home/lc/bin"}' \
    | node scripts/bun-runner.js scripts/worker-service.cjs hook claude-code context
# → first-run banner ("This project has no memory yet…")

$ echo '{"session_id":"t2","hook_event_name":"SessionStart","source":"compact","cwd":"/home/lc"}' \
    | node scripts/bun-runner.js scripts/worker-service.cjs hook claude-code context
# → full "[lc] recent context" injection, 50 observations
```

Identical payloads, one path component of difference, opposite outcomes. Sessions launched inside a real git repo resolve to the repo's own project on both paths and behave correctly — the mismatch is specific to the no-git-root fallback.

## Ruled out

- Worker down / capture broken / DB corruption — all disproven live (worker healthy, observations from the affected sessions present in the DB under the parent project).
- `CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR` — unset in these sessions either way; the read-path resolution doesn't consult it.

## Workaround we're running

Local patch to the cached scripts mapping any non-git-repo cwd under the home directory to the home project name, re-applied by a SessionStart hook after plugin updates. Works, but it's machine-specific and shouldn't be needed — mentioning it mainly to confirm that unifying the two resolutions does fix the observed behavior.

Not prescribing which side should be normalized to the other — the read path and write path each have history (#1819, #2663), and you have the full picture of what project identity should mean here. Happy to test a fix or provide more traces.
